title: Airbus launches new flight test programme for Wing of Tomorrow
excerpt: Farnborough, United Kingdom, 21 July 2026 – Airbus is launching a new flight-test campaign to evaluate the performance of high-span wings for next-generation single-aisle aircraft, marking the next phase of one of its largest research and technology programmes, Wing of Tomorrow.Over the next three years, Airbus will design, build and flight test full scale wing extensions. These will be installed on an A321neo aircraft and used to evaluate how the different wing geometries perform in real flight conditions.
content_html:
Farnborough, United Kingdom, 21 July 2026 – Airbus is launching a new flight-test campaign to evaluate the performance of high-span wings for next-generation single-aisle aircraft, marking the next phase of one of its largest research and technology programmes, Wing of Tomorrow.

Over the next three years, Airbus will design, build and flight test full scale wing extensions. These will be installed on an A321neo aircraft and used to evaluate how the different wing geometries perform in real flight conditions. 

The extensions, each measuring several metres, will replicate a folding wing in the fully extended position during a flight. They will be fitted with comprehensive testing equipment to capture behaviour in flight and evaluate how the longer wingspan impacts aircraft handling. 

“For more than 50 years, Airbus has spearheaded aviation innovation, continually pushing the boundaries of what’s possible to deliver more efficient aircraft for future generations. This is why the Wing of Tomorrow programme is such an exciting and rewarding journey to be on”, said Sue Partridge, Airbus Head of Wing of Tomorrow programme. “Importantly, the wing is one of the biggest levers we have to improve flight efficiency, which is why the Wing of Tomorrow is so critical for our next generation single aisle aircraft. This flight-test campaign will allow us to safely challenge traditional design limits and explore the benefits of longer wings.” 

Airbus is currently using advanced digital modelling and wind tunnel testing to finalise the designs of the wing extensions before they are tested in a representative flying environment. The flight data is critical to de-risk and unlock the full potential that longer wings could bring to next generation aircraft. 

The Wing of Tomorrow programme is centred around creating longer, lighter, and more slender wings to maximise aerodynamic efficiency and reduce fuel burn. The wing extensions will be assembled at Airbus’ Wing Technology Development Centre in the UK and flight tested in Toulouse.

Airbus has already built three 17-metre (ground based) wing demonstrators to explore the advantages of increased wingspan and the benefits of more than 100 manufacturing and assembly technologies.

Since 2014, the Wing of Tomorrow programme has been backed by £227million in funding from the Aerospace Technology Institute (ATI) programme, a partnership between the ATI, Department for Business and Trade and Innovate UK, designed to maintain the UK's position as a leader in aerospace technology. 

Note to editors:

  • Alongside the engine, the wing is one of the biggest levers to improve aircraft efficiency. Airbus experts constantly examine new ideas and technologies, taking their inspiration from nature and helping to further optimise wing design for the next generation of aircraft.
  • The Wing of Tomorrow is one of Airbus’ largest research and technology programmes, dedicated to designing and producing next-generation wing technology. It explores new wing designs to optimise aerodynamic performance, as well as new industrial processes to increase production rates and reduce costs.
  • In parallel, Airbus UpNext is accelerating the eXtra Performance WING demonstrator, focused on developing a wing that can change shape during flight to maximise aerodynamic efficiency. The assembly of this flight demonstrator is nearing completion in Cazaux, southern France, with a maiden flight (remotely piloted) planned by the end of 2026.
  • Both the Wing of Tomorrow and the eXtra Performance Wing demonstrators intend to advance wing technology for Airbus next generation single-aisle aircraft. 

@Airbus #Wings #WingofTomorrow #NGSA #FIA2026

blocks.html_en: • Both the Wing of Tomorrow and the eXtra Performance Wing demonstrators intend to advance wing technology for Airbus next generation single-aisle aircraft.

sentences.en: Both the Wing of Tomorrow and the eXtra Performance Wing demonstrators intend to advance wing technology for Airbus next generation single-aisle aircraft.
sentences.zh: 明日之翼和超高性能机翼演示器都旨在推进空客下一代单通道飞机的机翼技术。

content_html_zh:
英国法恩伯勒，2026 年 7 月 21 日——空中客车公司正在启动一项新的飞行测试活动，以评估下一代单通道飞机大跨度机翼的性能，这标志着其最大的研究和技术项目之一“明日之翼”进入下一阶段。

在接下来的三年里，空中客车公司将设计、建造和飞行测试全尺寸的机翼延伸部分。 这些将安装在 A321neo 飞机上，用于评估不同机翼几何形状在实际飞行条件下的表现。

每个延伸部分有几米长，将在飞行过程中复制处于完全伸展位置的折叠机翼。 它们将配备全面的测试设备，以捕获飞行中的行为并评估较长的翼展如何影响飞机的操控性。

“50多年来，空客一直引领航空创新，不断突破界限，为子孙后代提供更高效的飞机。 这就是为什么“明日之翼”计划是一次令人兴奋且收获颇丰的旅程。”空客“明日之翼”计划负责人 Sue Partridge 说道。 “重要的是，机翼是我们提高飞行效率的最大杠杆之一，这就是为什么‘明日之翼’对于我们下一代单通道飞机如此重要。 这次飞行测试活动将使我们能够安全地挑战传统设计限制并探索更长机翼的好处。”

空中客车公司目前正在使用先进的数字建模和风洞测试来完成机翼延伸部分的设计，然后再在代表性的飞行环境中进行测试。 飞行数据对于降低风险和释放更长机翼为下一代飞机带来的全部潜力至关重要。

明日之翼计划的重点是制造更长、更轻、更细长的机翼，以最大限度地提高空气动力效率并减少燃油消耗。 机翼延伸部分将在空客位于英国的机翼技术开发中心组装，并在图卢兹进行飞行测试。

空中客车公司已经建造了三个 17 米（地面）机翼演示机，以探索增加翼展的优势以及 100 多种制造和装配技术的优势。

自 2014 年以来，“明日之翼”计划得到了航空航天技术研究所 (ATI) 计划 2.27 亿英镑的资金支持，该计划是 ATI、商业贸易部和英国创新局之间的合作伙伴关系，旨在维持英国作为航空航天技术领导者的地位。

编辑注意：

  • 除了发动机之外，机翼也是提高飞机效率的最大杠杆之一。 空客专家不断研究新的想法和技术，从大自然中汲取灵感，帮助进一步优化下一代飞机的机翼设计。
  • 明日之翼是空客最大的研究和技术项目之一，致力于设计和生产下一代机翼技术。 它探索新的机翼设计以优化空气动力学性能，以及新的工业流程以提高生产率并降低成本。
  • 与此同时，空中客车UpNext正在加速eXtra Performance WING演示机的开发，重点是开发一种可以在飞行过程中改变形状的机翼，以最大限度地提高空气动力效率。 该飞行演示机在法国南部卡佐的组装已接近完成，计划于 2026 年底进行首次飞行（远程驾驶）。
  • 明日之翼和超高性能机翼演示器都旨在推进空客下一代单通道飞机的机翼技术。

@空客#Wings #WingofTomorrow #NGSA #FIA2026
